introduction: Database Management Systems (DBMS) are complex software applications designed to store, retrieve, and manage data efficiently. Understanding their underlying architecture is crucial for effective database design and administration.

sections.title: Three-Schema Architecture
sections.content: The three-schema architecture was proposed to achieve data independence. It separates the user applications and the physical database. The three levels are: 1. Internal Level (Physical schema) describing physical storage. 2. Conceptual Level (Logical schema) describing the structure of the whole database. 3. External Level (View schema) describing the part of the database a specific user group is interested in.

sections.title: Data Independence
sections.content: Data independence is the ability to modify a schema definition in one level without affecting a schema definition in the next higher level. There are two types: Logical Data Independence and Physical Data Independence.

sections.title: Client-Server Architecture
sections.content: Modern DBMS typically use a client-server architecture where the database server runs on a centralized machine, and client applications connect to it over a network to request data using protocols like TCP/IP.
